hints.junior: Before you paste an error into AI, write one sentence: what you think is wrong. That single step forces understanding and makes the model's answer 10× more useful.
hints.mid: The anti-pattern: paste error → accept fix → paste next error. The professional pattern: read the error → hypothesize → ask targeted question → verify the fix makes sense.
hints.staff: Dependency on AI for error resolution without understanding creates brittle systems. When the model is wrong (hallucination, wrong context), developers who didn't understand the error can't detect the bad fix.

hints.junior: Broken mode: paste a raw stack trace — watch the model give a generic fix. Fixed mode: add a one-sentence hypothesis — watch the answer become specific and correct.
hints.mid: The model in fixed mode asks a follow-up question. That's the signal you gave enough context to enter a diagnostic conversation rather than a copy-paste loop.
hints.staff: Notice the broken fix introduces a subtle regression — the model patched the symptom, not the root cause. That's the core failure mode: AI optimizes for the visible error, not for system correctness.

anecdotes.junior: I watched a junior dev fix the same 'Cannot read properties of undefined' error three times in one hour by pasting it into ChatGPT each time. Adding a null check was never their idea — they just accepted what came back.
anecdotes.mid: The best AI-assisted debugging sessions I've seen follow the rubber-duck pattern: describe the problem to yourself, then ask AI to validate your hypothesis. It's faster than open-ended 'fix this.'
anecdotes.staff: This is fundamentally a cognitive load problem, not an AI problem. Accepting patches without comprehension is the same failure mode as copying StackOverflow answers. AI makes it faster — and therefore worse for learning unless you add the deliberate comprehension step.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're looking at the most common bad habit with AI coding assistants — pasting errors without actually understanding them.
sections.mid: This case covers the diagnosis-first pattern for AI-assisted debugging: how to ask better questions, read error messages intentionally, and avoid the paste-fix-repeat loop.
sections.staff: We're covering the cognitive dependency risk, the hypothesis-validation loop, and how to use AI as an amplifier of understanding rather than a replacement for it.
